label: SES-061 Code Change Lifecycle methodology drafted: settled 8 design decisions (DEC-183..182), authored methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 and schema-design-kickoff-commit.md (PI-028 kickoff); PI-027 stays Open pending PI-033 retroactive resolve; 1 session, 8 decisions, 0 planning items, 11 references

session.title: Code Change Lifecycle methodology drafted: eight deferred design decisions settled, methodology document v1.0 authored, PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff produced
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai planning conversation. Opened against the kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, authored by predecessor SES-057. The kickoff established the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (per DEC-168 adopting Option III, first-class commit entities) and named eight design decisions deferred for settlement in this conversation: commit identifier strategy, granularity of resolves edges, commit ingestion trigger, commit record fields, multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping, typed addresses relationship_kind, work_ticket authoring rule scope, and direction/name of the blocks relationship_kind. The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default) and worked through the eight decisions in sequence using the consequential decision template, with each decision recommendation grounded in the actual state of the dogfood database (db-export snapshots queried via Python directly; no MCP server in this sandbox). Five of eight decisions ran full 8-element templates (#1 #2 #3 #5 #8); three were decide-and-announce because the options did not pass part-two of the two-part test (#4 commit fields, #6 addresses typed kind, #7 work_ticket scope rule — though #7 was settled with strong reference to the existing work_ticket spec's pre-authored canonical examples). Three artifacts produced: the methodology document, the PI-028 commit schema kickoff, and the close-out payload itself. The PI-027 resolution posture was settled in passing as option (a) — defer to PI-033 retroactive resolve — to keep PI-027 honest as a normative-but-not-yet-implemented planning item, preserving the diagnostic signal of 'planning items in Resolved status without resolves edges are data-integrity violations.'
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, opening this conversation against PI-027 to draft methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md and settle eight deferred design decisions, with the PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff as the closing deliverable.

Structured summary of the methodology drafting conversation:

(1) Identifier collision and three-step renumbering. The kickoff was authored by SES-057 anticipating SES-058 and DEC-171..178 for this conversation. The first rebase came when a parallel sandbox completed the audit feature v1.2 planning workstream and queued ses_058.json (claiming SES-058 + DEC-171..174 + PI-034..044); this conversation rebased to SES-059 + DEC-175..182. The second rebase came on push attempt — a parallel sandbox completed the PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill planning workstream and pushed first as ses_059.json (claiming SES-059 + DEC-175..177); this conversation rebased to SES-060 + DEC-178..185. The third rebase came on the next push attempt — yet another parallel sandbox completed the audit-v1.2 §9 resolutions conversation and pushed ses_060.json (claiming SES-060 + DEC-178..182); this conversation rebased to SES-061 + DEC-183..190. The final identifier assignments survived push verification, and applies must run in this order: SES-058 audit feature; SES-059 PI-024 backfill; SES-060 audit-v1.2 resolutions; SES-061 this conversation. Each apply requires its predecessors. The renumbering experience itself surfaces a methodology question for PI-032 to address: parallel-sandbox conversations cannot reserve identifier ranges in advance; reserve-at-apply-time is the only collision-free option. Doug acknowledged each rebase by proceeding.

(2) Decision 1 — commit identifier strategy. Three options framed: (a) CM-NNN sequence with SHA stored separately, (b) SHA-as-identifier with full 40-char SHA as primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier with SHA as unique secondary key plus /by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. Recommended option (a) refined to four-digit padding (CM-NNNN matching REF-NNNN's high-volume pattern) with the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint borrowed from option (c). Rationale: uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability; the convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally. Doug confirmed.

(3) Decision 2 — granularity of resolves edges. Three options framed: (a) per-commit only, (b) per-conversation only, (c) both. Recommended option (b): single resolves kind for conversation-to-planning_item, with multi-conversation work handled via a separate addresses kind (extended to conversation-to-planning_item as well as work_ticket-to-planning_item in Decision 6). Rationale: per-commit grain forces an authoring judgment Doug should not have to make at close-out time ('did this kickoff commit really contribute or just the methodology commit?'); per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided and at which planning_item.status transitions. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine. Unique constraint on (target_type, target_id, relationship_kind='resolves') ensures a planning_item is resolved by at most one conversation. Doug confirmed.

(4) Decision 3 — commit ingestion trigger. Four options framed: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling, (d) apply-time git scan. Surfaced a fifth option (a-prime): payload-declared with mechanical enumeration helper at close-out authoring time — combines auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d). Recommended (a-prime): the close-out payload contains a commits array enumerating each commit by SHA; a helper command (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) runs at close-out authoring time to populate the section from 'git log <last-applied-sha>..HEAD' per touched repo. No hooks, no polling, no apply-time scanning. The 'last applied SHA' is derived per repo by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1; no separate state table needed. Apply-script idempotency: UNIQUE on commit_sha rejects duplicates with HTTP 409. Doug confirmed.

(5) Decision 4 — commit record fields. Did not pass two-part test (impact real but per-field options are 'include or defer'; outcomes don't materially differ). Decided and announced as a batch: v0.8 fields are commit_identifier, commit_sha (UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_committed_at (from %cI), commit_repository, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas (JSON array — replaces kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' to handle merge commits cleanly), commit_files_changed_count, commit_conversation_id (FK), plus governance-standard timestamps. Deferred to v0.9: commit_files_changed_paths, commit_signed_by, commit_committer_name/email, commit_message_trailers. The 'parent_shas as JSON array' refinement of the kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' was a side recommendation accepted in the same turn. Doug confirmed.

(6) Decision 5 — multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping. Four options framed: (a) repo-scoped commits with cross-engagement references, (a-prime) conversation-scoped commits (all live in the conversation's engagement DB with repository column distinguishing physical repo), (b) per-engagement child records, (c) shared meta-DB. Recommended (a-prime). Rationale: engagements are accounting units; every commit lives with its conversation; no cross-engagement references for commits; no new shared-DB pattern. Edge case named: a CBM-engagement conversation that incidentally commits in crmbuilder (rare — dogfood scope but possible) lives in CBM engagement DB regardless. PI-033 back-fill scope: each historical commit assigned to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; the commit goes in that engagement. A cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces. Doug confirmed.

(7) Decision 6 — typed addresses relationship_kind. Did not pass two-part test (the functional outcomes of 'addresses' vs 'is_about with type filters' don't materially differ; only the query-clarity reading differs). Decided and announced. Option (a): add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting two source/target pairs — (work_ticket, planning_item) (the kickoff's narrow case) and (conversation, planning_item) (the multi-conversation case from Decision 2). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides. Existing v0.7 backfill's eight work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges migrate to addresses as part of PI-033. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has named query patterns and a committed use site in this workstream, so deferral test does not trigger. Doug confirmed.

(8) Decision 7 — work_ticket authoring rule scope. Three options framed: (a) narrow (Claude Code prompts only, ~168 historical), (b) middle (Claude Code plus kickoff/planning-prompt files, ~195 historical), (c) broad (every single-use seed file including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-*, ~221 historical). Recommended (c) broad. Rationale: the work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind closed enum has values kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other — designed for all three categories; the spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories; the spec author intent was already broad. Filename pattern classifies: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; other → other. Back-fill cost: ~213 net new records under (c) versus ~160 under (a) — half-day to one-day difference; not material. Going-forward enforcement is documented in PI-032's methodology rollout. Doug confirmed.

(9) Decision 8 — direction and name of the blocks relationship_kind. Three options framed: (a) keep blocks with 'source blocks target = target waits for source' convention (natural English; existing two rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only kind value migrates; semantics match natural English with no convention to memorize). Recommended (c) blocked_by. Rationale: a self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge; conventions that disagree with natural English get violated in the long run. The two existing rows REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025) keep endpoints; their kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. Folded into PI-029's Alembic migration. Doug confirmed.

(10) PI-027 resolution posture. Per kickoff section 'Close-out expectations,' two options for handling this conversation's own resolution given the payload format gap: (a) defer (leave PI-027 Open in this close-out; PI-033 resolves it retroactively once resolves_planning_items payload section ships in PI-030), or (b) hand-flip PI-027's status via direct API after apply. Methodology section 9 adopts (a). Rationale: the honesty of 'the methodology specifies a model the schema does not yet support' matters more than the visual neatness of a flipped status; the diagnostic that motivated the workstream ('PI-022 is the only Resolved planning item, and it has no resolves edge') would be impossible to detect under (b); adopting (a) preserves the diagnostic signal through this conversation's tail.

(11) Methodology document authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 (this conversation) is the design authority for the seven-stage Code Change Lifecycle chain. Nine sections: Purpose and Scope; the Seven-Stage Lifecycle (planning item → planning conversation → execution conversation opens against work_ticket → execution conversation produces commits → close-out records resolves/addresses/work_tickets/commits → apply transaction lands records → downstream queries walk chain); Vocabulary Additions (commit entity type, resolves and addresses kinds, blocks→blocked_by rename); Close-out Payload Format Extension (four new sections: commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items; fixed apply order); Authoring Rules (when work_ticket is born/consumed/superseded; when planning_item is resolved; commit ingestion at close-out time; attribution rule for manual commits between close-outs); Audit Query Patterns (five canonical SQL examples covering PI→commits, commit→PIs, workstream→commits, indeterminate-state detection, work_ticket→PIs); Migration and Back-fill Posture (PI-033 scope: ~221 historical work_tickets, several hundred commits, retroactive resolves edges, two is_about-to-addresses migrations, two blocks-to-blocked_by rows); Known Gaps and Downstream Dependencies (PI-028 through PI-033 mapped to deliverables; methodology is normative-but-not-yet-implemented until PI-030 ships); PI-027 Resolution Posture (option a — defer).

(12) PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md is the work_ticket seeding the PI-028 schema-design conversation. The kickoff intentionally defers most architectural questions to the methodology document and surfaces only the rendering-into-schema-spec-format questions: lifecycle posture (documentary), cross-spec precedent contributions (three candidates: by-natural-key lookup pattern, JSON array column for variable-cardinality scalars, conversation-scoped accounting unit principle), field naming convention reaffirmation (parent-prefix per DEC-046), validation rules for natural-key columns (commit_sha 40-hex-lowercase, commit_repository non-empty), relationship rendering (commit_conversation_id as FK only, no typed refs kind per DEC-133 deferral test), acceptance criteria specific to commit, and derived endpoint candidates. Read list prioritizes methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md as authoritative; references work_ticket.md and reference_book.md as closest precedents; cross-references the spec-design template. Close-out expectations for PI-028 named: 3-6 decisions expected, PI-028 stays Open per the same PI-027 logic, and authoring the PI-029 Prompt A is named as an in-flight follow-on.

(13) Sandbox commit and push. The methodology document, PI-028 kickoff, ses_061.json close-out payload, and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-061.md apply prompt are committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention.
session.artifacts_produced: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 (the design authority for the seven-stage Code Change Lifecycle chain); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md (work_ticket seeding the PI-028 commit entity schema-design conversation); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_061.json (this payload); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-061.md (apply prompt). Eight decisions DEC-183 through DEC-190. Zero new planning items (PI-028 through PI-033 already exist from SES-057). Eleven references: eight decided_in linking each decision to SES-061, one is_about from SES-061 to PI-028 (next planning item in the workstream sequence), one is_about from SES-061 to the methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md reference_book (note: that reference_book record will be authored by PI-033 backfill, not in this conversation; the reference here is forward-declared by identifier — see in-flight note), one supersedes from this conversation to SES-057 (this conversation succeeds SES-057 in the workstream's conversation chain).
session.in_flight_at_end: Code Change Lifecycle workstream now in its second phase: methodology-authored, schema-spec-pending. The next conversation opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md to produce PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/commit.md per the seven-section governance schema spec format. PI-028 stays Open at the end of that conversation (same payload-format-gap reason as PI-027). After PI-028 closes, PI-029 schema migration work begins as a Claude Code execution series; the first prompt CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-A-commits-table-and-vocab.md is authored by PI-028's close-out or by a fresh planning conversation depending on PI-028's time-and-context budget. Subsequent planning items PI-030 (close-out payload extension and apply script update and enumerate_commits helper), PI-031 (Commits panel UI), PI-032 (methodology rollout including CLAUDE.md updates), PI-033 (historical backfill including this PI-027 retroactive resolve) execute in strict dependency order per DEC-170. Out-of-scope items deferred per methodology section 1: workstreams that produce no commits stay outside the lifecycle's audit-chain scope; cross-engagement commit queries beyond the per-engagement boundary stay deferred unless a use case surfaces.

decisions.title: Commit entity identifier strategy: CM-NNNN with full SHA in a separate commit_sha field plus /by-sha/{sha} convenience lookup
decisions.context: The Code Change Lifecycle workstream introduces the commit entity as a first-class governance record. The kickoff named three identifier-strategy options: (a) CM-NNN sequence-style identifier with SHA stored as a separate field, (b) SHA-as-identifier with the full or stable-prefix SHA as the primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier per the prefixed-identifier convention plus SHA as a unique secondary key with a /commits/by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. The decision shapes how every future query, prose discussion, apply-prompt reference, and audit-chain walk refers to commits.
decisions.decision: Option (a) refined to four-digit padding plus the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint from option (c). Commit identifier is CM-NNNN (four-digit zero-padded sequence; matches REF-NNNN's high-volume four-digit pattern; will not need bumping for at least 10,000 commits). The full 40-character SHA is stored in a separate commit_sha column with UNIQUE INDEX. A GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint handles full or prefix-SHA lookups; prefix ambiguity returns HTTP 409 with the candidate list. Client-side identifier assignment per the existing prefixed-identifier convention (crmbuilder CLAUDE.md line 62).
decisions.rationale: Uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability. Every other governance entity uses the same NNN-prefixed shape; breaking the pattern for one entity type asks every reader to remember which entity gets which identifier shape. The /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally; routine audit-chain queries join through the CM-NNNN identifier and never see the SHA-translation hop. The four-digit choice (over three-digit CM-NNN) acknowledges that commits will be the highest-volume governance entity in the system and the four-digit padding aligns with REF-NNNN's already-established precedent.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (b) SHA-as-identifier rejected — breaks the NNN-prefix uniformity for marginal gain; SHA ordering is hash-random not chronological; identifier-comparable queries (e.g., 'CM-101 came before CM-102') would not work. Option (c) full both-canonical rejected — having two equally-canonical references to one record forces every downstream code site to pick which is canonical for its use, multiplying complexity. Three-digit CM-NNN rejected on the volume argument; even a single moderately active workstream produces dozens of commits.
decisions.consequences: Every commit record carries a four-digit CM-NNNN identifier assigned client-side at apply time. Audit-chain queries return CM-NNNN values; downstream code refers to commits by CM-NNNN in prose. The /by-sha/{sha} endpoint covers SHA-first callers (notably the git-log ingestion helper specified in DEC-185 and the future commits-panel UI). The high-volume nature of commits (versus other governance entities in the low hundreds) is acknowledged in the schema by choosing four-digit padding from inception.

decisions.title: Granularity of resolves edges: per-conversation only, with multi-conversation contribution via addresses
decisions.context: A conversation that delivers a planning item commits possibly N times to deliver it. The resolution-declaration edge can live at commit grain (commit-to-planning_item), conversation grain (conversation-to-planning_item), or both. The choice shapes payload authoring discipline, edge volume, and the meaning of planning_item.status='Resolved'.
decisions.decision: Per-conversation only. Single resolves relationship_kind admitting the pair (conversation, planning_item). Multi-conversation work — where work on a planning_item spans multiple conversations and only the final one resolves — uses a separate addresses kind (per DEC-188) admitting (conversation, planning_item) for the non-terminal contributing conversations. The resolves edge is unique per planning_item (UNIQUE on target_type+target_id filtered by relationship_kind='resolves'); a planning_item can be resolved by at most one conversation. The 'commits that contributed to PI-NNN' query walks refs(relationship_kind IN ('resolves','addresses') AND target=PI-NNN AND source_type='conversation') then joins to commits via the commit_conversation_id FK.
decisions.rationale: Per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided in a close-out payload, matches how planning_item.status transitions (one event per planning_item, not N), and avoids an authoring discipline judgment ('did this kickoff commit really contribute to PI-NNN or just the methodology commit?') that should not be required at close-out time. Per-commit grain forces that judgment; both-grain doubles edge volume for marginal gain. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Per-commit only — accepts the authoring discipline cost in exchange for direct SQL; rejected on the discipline-burden argument and the resolved-status-overstating problem (10 contributing commits would suggest 10 'resolutions' when only one delivery happened). Both grains — accepts doubled edge volume for fastest SQL on both query directions; rejected as redundant since both directions are queryable under the per-conversation model with one extra join.
decisions.consequences: Apply transaction generates exactly one resolves edge per resolved planning_item and atomically flips planning_item.status from Open to Resolved in the same transaction. Manual status flips outside the resolves-edge mechanism are forbidden by methodology (per methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md section 5.4). The diagnostic 'planning_items in Resolved status without a resolves edge' becomes a data-integrity violation signal that the diagnostic script flags. Multi-conversation contribution is captured via addresses edges (DEC-188) without doubling commit-level edge volume.

decisions.title: Commit ingestion trigger: payload-declared, helper-enumerated via git log at close-out authoring time
decisions.context: Commits exist in git first; their governance records are created somewhere in the close-out lifecycle. The kickoff named four options: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling against git log, (d) apply-time git scan. A fifth option (a-prime) surfaced during the decision: payload-declared like (a), but enumerated mechanically via a helper command at close-out authoring time — combines (a)'s auditability with (d)'s no-miss quality.
decisions.decision: Option (a-prime). The close-out payload contains a top-level commits array. The conversation's close-out turn runs a helper (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or an inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) to populate the array from 'git log <last-applied-sha-per-repo>..HEAD' for each repo the conversation touched. The 'last-applied SHA per repo' is derived at helper-run time by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1 — no separate state table. apply_close_out.py POSTs each commits-array entry as a commit record. The commit's commit_conversation_id is set to the close-out's owning conversation by the apply script; no explicit field needed in the payload entry. Idempotency is enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on commit_sha (HTTP 409 on duplicate; per-record error tolerance allows apply to continue).
decisions.rationale: Combining auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d) eliminates the failure modes of each: forgotten-enumeration of pure (a), API-down or sandbox-unhookable of (b), latency-and-cursor-state of (c), repo-state-divergence-at-apply of pure (d). The cost is one mechanical step at close-out authoring time and ~150 bytes of payload per commit. The 'last applied SHA' state lives implicitly in the commits table itself, requiring no new state machinery.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (a) raw manual enumeration — rejected on forgotten-commit silent-gap failure mode. Option (b) git hook — rejected on commit-time API-coupling and sandbox-unhookability of sandbox commits. Option (c) polling — rejected on latency, polling-state, and close-out-timing awkwardness. Option (d) apply-time git scan without payload declaration — rejected because apply non-determinism if repo state diverges between conversation close and apply time (rebase, force-push) silently changes what gets ingested.
decisions.consequences: Every close-out turn ends with a helper invocation that emits the commits payload section. The helper is implemented in PI-030's scope along with the close-out payload schema extension and apply script update. Until PI-030 ships, conversation close-outs continue to use the v0.7 payload format without the commits section. The helper specification includes per-repo bracketing, accommodating the conversation-scoped multi-repo case from DEC-187. Attribution rule: every commit in the enumerated range is attributed to the close-out's owning conversation, regardless of physical author identity; manual commits Doug made between close-outs are rolled into the next conversation's set. If attribution discipline becomes a problem, a future v0.9 adds an optional commit_author_kind field.

decisions.title: Commit record fields for v0.8: thirteen-column shape with parent_shas as JSON array; four candidate fields deferred to v0.9
decisions.context: The kickoff named a minimum field set (sha, message, author, author_email, committed_at, repository, branch, parent_sha) and an optional candidate set (files_changed_count, files_changed_paths, signed_by, committer, merge_parent_sha). The decision settles which optional fields are in v0.8 versus deferred to v0.9.
decisions.decision: v0.8 commit record carries: commit_identifier (CM-NNNN, client-side), commit_sha (TEXT UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line (TEXT), commit_message_full (TEXT), commit_author_name (TEXT), commit_author_email (TEXT), commit_committed_at (TIMESTAMP from %cI committer date), commit_repository (TEXT), commit_branch (TEXT), commit_parent_shas (JSON array; 0 entries for initial commit, 1 for normal, 2 for merge — replaces kickoff's singular parent_sha to handle merges cleanly), commit_files_changed_count (INTEGER from git diff-tree), commit_conversation_id (FK to conversation.conversation_identifier), plus the three governance-standard timestamps commit_created_at, commit_updated_at, commit_deleted_at. Deferred to v0.9 or later: commit_files_changed_paths (separate normalized table when needed), commit_signed_by (Doug's commits aren't signed), commit_committer_name and commit_committer_email (practically identical to author in this workflow), commit_message_trailers (parsed Co-authored-by/Signed-off-by — not used today).
decisions.rationale: The chosen set covers every audit-chain query named in the methodology document section 6 without unnecessary columns. The kickoff's singular parent_sha was replaced with the JSON-array parent_shas because merge commits have two parents; a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly without a separate merge_parent_sha column. Deferred fields are speculative — commit_files_changed_paths has no current query pattern (git show <sha> provides on demand) and would either need a large JSON blob or a separate normalized table (more migration than the value justifies); the rest are either null on every row in this workflow or duplicate fields already covered by author_name and author_email.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Including commit_files_changed_paths in v0.8 — rejected for migration cost without query-pattern motivation. Including commit_committer_name/email — rejected because the values are almost always identical to author in this workflow. Including commit_signed_by — rejected because Doug's commits aren't currently signed and the field would be null on every row. Keeping the kickoff's singular parent_sha — rejected because merge commits have two parents and a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly.
decisions.consequences: PI-029 ships the Alembic migration creating the commits table with these thirteen plus three columns. The enumerate_commits.py helper emits exactly the v0.8 set; payload entries match the column set. Adding any deferred field is a future v0.8.x or v0.9 migration. The parent_shas JSON-array column is the first JSON-array column on a governance entity table; if it works cleanly, future schemas needing variable-cardinality scalar lists may adopt the same pattern (named as a candidate cross-spec precedent for PI-028 to elevate in the commit schema spec section 7).

decisions.title: Multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping: conversation-scoped commits with repository column distinguishing physical repo
decisions.context: V2 engagement databases are per-client (CRMBUILDER dogfood DB, CBM client DB). A conversation may produce commits in multiple repos in a single session — for example, a dogfood conversation that fixes a deploy-engine bug in crmbuilder and also commits a YAML update in ClevelandBusinessMentoring to validate the fix. The kickoff named three options: (a) repo-scoped commits per engagement DB (each engagement tracks its own repo's commits) with cross-engagement references, (b) per-engagement child conversation records (parent in one DB, mirror in each engagement), (c) shared meta-DB for commits across all engagements.
decisions.decision: Conversation-scoped commits (option a-prime, surfaced during the decision). Every commit lives in the same engagement DB as the conversation that produced it, regardless of which physical repo holds the SHA. The repository column on each commit row (TEXT, no enum) records which physical repo holds the SHA. Cross-engagement reference mechanics for commits are not introduced. A future cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap to add (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces.
decisions.rationale: Engagements are accounting units; every record produced by a conversation belongs to that conversation's engagement. The per-engagement DB isolation pattern stays self-contained. Audit-chain queries inside an engagement stay one-DB; no cross-DB walks needed. The edge case 'a CBM-engagement conversation incidentally commits in crmbuilder' (rare — dogfood scope, but possible) is handled: the commit lives in CBM engagement DB with repository='crmbuilder'. The cost — CBM engagement DB never sees CBM-repo commits made under a CRMBUILDER-engagement conversation — is acceptable because such cross-engagement commit queries are not on the horizon; engagement-scoped audit queries are the only operational pattern.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (a) repo-scoped with cross-engagement references — rejected because it requires introducing cross-DB reference mechanics not currently used; cross-engagement references add persistent complexity at every query site for marginal gain. Option (b) per-engagement child records — rejected because parent-child conversations across DBs make conversation completeness ambiguous and double or triple conversation records per cross-repo work. Option (c) shared meta-DB — rejected because it introduces a new shared-DB pattern that doesn't exist elsewhere in V2; the per-engagement isolation is the established model.
decisions.consequences: Every engagement DB's commits table tracks commits across all repos its conversations touched. The repository column distinguishes physical repo. PI-033 back-fill assigns each historical commit to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; most historical commits are crmbuilder dogfood work and go to the CRMBUILDER engagement DB; CBM-engagement-only commits go to CBM. The 'conversation as accounting unit' principle is the candidate cross-spec precedent the PI-028 schema spec can elevate for future external-artifact entity types (any entity representing artifacts physically outside the engagement DB inherits the principle).

decisions.title: Add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item)
decisions.context: Work_tickets are born to address planning items; today this is captured as a generic is_about edge with no semantic specificity. DEC-184's per-conversation resolves model also requires a non-terminal contribution edge for multi-conversation work where a conversation contributes but doesn't resolve. Both use sites share the same semantic: 'source is doing work toward target without necessarily completing it.' The kickoff named (a) add addresses as a typed kind versus (b) stay with is_about and rely on type filters for queries.
decisions.decision: Option (a) — add addresses as a typed relationship_kind. Admissible source/target pairs: (work_ticket, planning_item) for the work_ticket-is-born-to-address case (work_ticket spec already anticipates this via the addresses_planning_item field on the work_ticket payload entry), and (conversation, planning_item) for the multi-conversation non-terminal contribution case (DEC-184). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides: a work_ticket can address multiple planning_items (cross-cutting tickets like the methodology document that informs PI-028 through PI-033); a planning_item can be addressed by multiple work_tickets (decomposed work); a conversation can address multiple planning_items; a planning_item can be addressed by multiple non-terminal conversations.
decisions.rationale: The query pattern 'all work_tickets created to address PI-NNN' is central to the audit-chain motivation in DEC-168, and DEC-184 already committed addresses to the conversation-side for multi-conversation resolution. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has two named use sites in this workstream and concrete query patterns, so the deferral test does not trigger. The query-clarity win (relationship_kind='addresses' reads obviously versus is_about filtered by source/target types) justifies the small vocabulary cost.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (b) stay with is_about — works functionally (queries filter is_about by source_type and target_type) but loses the semantic explicitness that addresses provides; future readers of an is_about edge from a work_ticket to a planning_item have to know the convention 'work_ticket is_about planning_item means addresses'. Adding addresses removes the convention-memorization burden.
decisions.consequences: vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS adds 'addresses'; _kinds_for_pair adds 'addresses' to the allowed kinds for (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item). The Alembic migration in PI-029's scope adds 'addresses' to the refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. The v0.7 backfill authored 8 work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges; PI-033 migrates those edges from kind='is_about' to kind='addresses'. The conversation-to-planning_item addresses pair is brand new and has no backfill obligation — future multi-conversation workstreams use it from inception.

decisions.title: Work_ticket authoring rule: every single-use seed document committed to either repo is a work_ticket, classified by the four-value kind enum
decisions.context: The work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind enum has four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other. The kickoff named three options for the authoring rule's scope: (a) narrow — only CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files become work_tickets (~168 historical), (b) middle — Claude Code prompts plus all kickoff/planning-prompt files (~195), (c) broad — every single-use seed including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* (~221). The work_ticket spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories, indicating the spec author's intent was already broad.
decisions.decision: Option (c) broad. Every single-use seed document committed to either repo is born as a work_ticket record in the same close-out payload that produces the commit. Filename pattern classifies by kind: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; anything else qualifying as a single-use seed → other (escape hatch). The work_ticket's addresses_planning_item (when known) names the planning item the conversation will address; for workstream-establishing kickoffs the addresses edge may be omitted and added later when the workstream's planning items are authored.
decisions.rationale: The work_ticket spec's kind enum was authored with kickoff_prompt as a first-class value precisely because the spec author intended kickoffs to be governed as work_tickets; option (a) would leave that enum value mostly unused for new work. The audit-chain motivation in DEC-168 wants to walk from a planning item through the discrete units of work that addressed it; excluding Claude.ai kickoff conversations from work_ticket governance creates an arbitrary boundary that omits most of the project's decision-making work. Back-fill cost difference between (c) and (a) is ~50 records — a half-day to one-day difference, not material.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (a) narrow rejected on the unused-enum-value and audit-chain-completeness arguments. Option (b) middle rejected as inconsistent — if kickoffs are work_tickets, session prompts and update prompts (structurally identical single-use seeds) should be too; drawing the line at filename patterns is arbitrary.
decisions.consequences: PI-033 back-fill authors ~221 work_ticket records: ~168 claude_code_prompt-kind, ~27 kickoff_prompt-kind, ~26 ad_hoc_prompt-kind. Each linked to its consuming conversation via conversation_opens_against_work_ticket where derivable from filename conventions; some require per-record judgment. PI-032 methodology rollout adds the authoring rule to the close-out checklist (every conversation that commits a seed file authors a work_ticket record in its close-out payload). Going forward, the close-out payload's work_tickets array becomes routine; the apply script processes it before the planning_items array per the fixed apply order in methodology section 4.

decisions.title: Rename relationship_kind blocks to blocked_by; existing two rows keep source/target endpoints and migrate kind value only
decisions.context: The v0.7 backfill created two blocks edges in the dogfood database: REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025). PI-024 is Phase 2 backfill of prior workstreams, PI-025 is Phase 3 backfill of prior conversations, PI-026 is Phase 4 backfill of historical applies. Natural ordering: PI-024 must finish before PI-025, which must finish before PI-026. The kickoff named three options: (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention 'source blocks target = target waits for source' (existing rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention 'source is blocked by target' (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only the kind name migrates; semantics match natural English directly).
decisions.decision: Option (c) — rename blocks to blocked_by. Admissible pair: (planning_item, planning_item). Semantics: source planning_item cannot proceed until target planning_item is resolved. Reads natively: 'PI-Y is blocked_by PI-X' means PI-Y is the dependent, PI-X is the prerequisite. The two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 keep their source/target endpoints unchanged; only the relationship_kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. The migration is one UPDATE statement folded into PI-029's consolidated Alembic migration alongside the resolves and addresses kind additions.
decisions.rationale: A self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge. Conventions that disagree with natural English (option b) get violated in the long run; swapping the existing rows (option a) creates a one-time correction obligation and keeps the ambiguous 'blocks' name forever. blocked_by reads directly in both authoring ('declare PI-Y blocked_by PI-X') and querying ('list every planning_item that is blocked_by something'). Two existing rows is a negligible migration cost.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention plus row swap — rejected because keeping the ambiguous 'blocks' name preserves future confusion. Option (b) keep blocks with inverted convention — rejected because future authors must memorize the inverted convention; conventions that fight natural English get violated. The alternative kind name 'depends_on' was also considered (more generic, slightly less specific to planning-workflow semantics); blocked_by was preferred because it directly evokes the dependency lifecycle (the dependent thing is the source, waiting on a prerequisite).
decisions.consequences: PI-029's Alembic migration drops the existing refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint, adds 'resolves' and 'addresses' and 'blocked_by' to the allowed set, removes 'blocks' from the allowed set, re-adds the CHECK constraint, then UPDATE refs SET relationship_kind='blocked_by' WHERE relationship_kind='blocks'. vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS replaces 'blocks' with 'blocked_by'; _kinds_for_pair for (planning_item, planning_item) admits 'blocked_by' (typed) plus 'supersedes' (same-type rule) plus the generic kinds. Two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 see their kind value flip in the migration; their source and target endpoints stay exactly as they are now, and they now read correctly as 'PI-025 is blocked_by PI-024' and 'PI-026 is blocked_by PI-025.'
